findings.location: disable_comments_settings() line 1265, delete_comments_settings() line 1391, get_sub_sites() line 1199
findings.reachability: Authenticated users with manage_options or manage_network_plugins capability who can trigger any of the three wp_ajax_ handlers.
findings.justification: All three AJAX handlers — get_sub_sites, disable_comments_save_settings (settings save), and disable_comments_delete_comments (bulk comment deletion) — verify against the identical nonce action string 'disable_comments_save_settings'. A nonce created for the read-only get_sub_sites call is valid for the destructive delete-all-comments call; nonce specificity provides no action-level isolation between handlers.

detection_pattern.anti_pattern: Each action string appears only once across all wp_verify_nonce calls for different destructive vs. read handlers
detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF/nonce: shared nonce action across handlers of different privilege levels
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_verify_nonce calls; when multiple handlers (especially read vs. destructive) share the same action string, a nonce obtained for one operation is silently accepted by the other, defeating per-action CSRF specificity.

findings.reachability: Authenticated users with manage_network_plugins (multisite) or manage_options (single-site) who call the wp_ajax_get_sub_sites handler.
findings.justification: The GET parameters pageSize and pageNumber are processed with sanitize_text_field(), which strips HTML but does not coerce to integer or enforce positive bounds. They are then used directly in arithmetic ($offset = ($pageNumber - 1) * $pageSize) and passed as the 'number' and 'offset' arguments to get_sites(). Passing pageSize=0 causes get_sites() to fetch all subsites in a single query (WordPress treats 0 as 'no limit'), enabling resource exhaustion against large networks; non-numeric values are silently cast by PHP, producing undefined pagination behaviour.

detection_pattern.section_name: Input validation: numeric pagination params validated as text
detection_pattern.description: Catches GET parameters intended to be integers (page, limit, offset, number, size) that are sanitized with sanitize_text_field instead of absint/intval; these are then used in arithmetic or query construction, risking DoS via unbounded queries or type-confusion bugs.

findings.reachability: Any user who can insert a comment record with a crafted comment_type value (e.g. via the WordPress REST API, XML-RPC, or direct database access through another plugin); payload executes when an administrator uses the Delete Comments admin UI and the front-end JavaScript renders the returned 'message' JSON field with innerHTML or equivalent.
findings.justification: At line 1111, comment_type values are fetched raw from the database without sanitization. At line 1123 the raw value is embedded directly into the display label: ucwords(str_replace('_', ' ', $value)) . ' (' . $value . ')'. This label is stored in $commenttypes[$value] and later inserted verbatim into the $message string at line 1433, then returned through wp_send_json_success(). If the admin-facing JavaScript renders the 'message' field using innerHTML (common in jQuery-based admin plugins), a comment_type such as '' stored in the database would execute in the administrator's browser. The file does not escape comment_type values before embedding them in the message.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: raw DB comment_type used in admin response label without escaping
detection_pattern.description: Finds direct database queries that SELECT comment_type from the comments table; when the retrieved value is used to build a display label or JSON message without esc_html/esc_attr, an attacker who can store a crafted comment_type achieves stored XSS in the admin context when the value is later rendered.

findings.location: is_network_admin_ajax_context() lines 133-144, disable_comments_settings() line 1267, delete_comments_settings() line 1393
findings.reachability: Any authenticated user with manage_options capability on a subsite in a multisite installation where the plugin is network-activated and sitewide_settings is disabled.
findings.justification: is_network_admin_ajax_context() returns true when the user-supplied GET parameter ?is_network_admin=1 is present on an AJAX request (line 140-143). In disable_comments_settings and delete_comments_settings, $is_network_ctx from this function controls both capability selection (is_network_ctx=true → require manage_network_plugins) and options-table routing (is_network_ctx=true → write to update_site_option). The code docblock at lines 126-132 acknowledges the parameter is 'forgeable'. While the actual capability gate correctly requires manage_network_plugins when is_network_ctx=true — so a plain manage_options user cannot use this to grant themselves super-admin writes — the forgeable routing hint could be abused in a confused-deputy scenario if future code paths do not uniformly re-check capabilities after trusting the routing flag. The more concrete risk today: a subsite admin omitting the ?is_network_admin=1 param when sitewide_settings='1' would fall into the 'manage_options' branch instead of the 'manage_network_plugins' branch, because the sitewide_settings guard only activates when networkactive AND sitewide_settings==='1', meaning a site admin on a network-activated install where sitewide_settings is not strictly '1' can write to their subsite's options with only manage_options.

detection_pattern.section_name: Access control: user-controlled routing flag used for context detection without pairing with capability check
detection_pattern.description: Finds GET parameter 'is_network_admin' being read as a routing hint; when this value influences which code branch executes without an adjacent current_user_can('manage_network_plugins') check, it creates a confused-deputy or privilege-escalation path.
